content:
Does the kernel unwind rust on panics? Or does it directly call into the kernel oops handler, which I assume just discards the stack frames of the kernel stack of a task.

I wonder whether it makes sense to add panic-protectors in rust code, which ensure invariants if callbacks panic. If unwinding is not performed at all, I can skip those.
There is no unwinding. Panics call BUG()
David Rheinsberg has marked this topic as resolved.

I wonder whether it makes sense to add panic-protectors in rust code, which ensure invariants if callbacks panic. If unwinding is not performed at all, I can skip those.

If it is not too much effort to make your code unwind safe, please still make them so. Unwinding path is going to be optimized away anyway.

If your code relies on absence of unwinding, please document so, because the code would be otherwise unsound outside kernel's environment.

I think it would be safer that we ask to document which Rust modules are written with unwind in mind (possibly because they are intended to be used outside the kernel as well), since otherwise there will likely be code out there that is not written with unwind in mind.

This is really only applicable to self-contained utilities, right? I mean, anything that uses even the most basic kernel APIs will be easily incompatible with user-space Rust. For instance, Arc in the kernel is always pinned, but that does not easily translate to user-space Rust. I mean, the same applies to the C code in the kernel. It is just quite hard to reliably share code.
